headlines.title: A closer look: Office spaces filling up in the Bay Area
headlines.description: After a long downturn started during the pandemic lockdown, Bay Area office spaces are getting filled up thanks largely to tech companies. Latest data shows office vacancies are dropping all over the Bay Area as companies — from artificial intelligence startups to large chip companies like AMD — look to grow. Business and tech reporter Scott Budman examines the data by JLL and VTS, two companies that track commercial real estate, in the video above. San Francisco Jul 7 Real Estate Jun 18 Bay Area Housing Jan 6 $524,000 annual salary needed to buy median-priced home in this Bay Area county

headlines.title: Apple files lawsuit accusing ChatGPT maker OpenAI of stealing trade secrets
headlines.description: Apple on Friday accused OpenAI of stealing trade secrets as it seeks to build its own hardware for ChatGPT, a major rupture in a partnership between the iPhone maker and the artificial intelligence company. Apple said in the lawsuit filed in a California federal court that OpenAI encouraged Apple employees it was recruiting to share confidential information, even guiding how to avoid scrutiny when taking jobs at the other company. “This case is about Apple’s former employees stealing Apple’s trade secrets for the benefit of OpenAI,” the filing says. “Apple brings this suit to put a stop to it.” Two former Apple employees who now work for OpenAI are also named as defendants. One is Tang Tan, who helped design the iPhone, Apple Watch and iPod and is now OpenAI’s chief hardware officer. The other is Chang Liu, a former electrical engineer Apple says it entrusted with some of its most sensitive product development efforts before Liu left Apple to join OpenAI earlier this year. OpenAI said it is still reviewing the filing, but spokesperson Drew Pusateri said in a statement Friday that OpenAI has “no interest in other companies’ trade secrets. We remain focused on building innovative technology that empowers people everywhere.” The suit accuses OpenAI of seeking shortcuts on hardware OpenAI has never said exactly what type of device it is building, but has described it as an effort to find a new way to interact with AI that goes beyond “traditional products and interfaces.” It’s part of a broader push to create a physical embodiment of the latest AI advances, a decade after Amazon and Google introduced screen-free talking speakers into homes. The lawsuit claims the effort was built partly on knowledge stolen from Apple. “OpenAI’s nascent hardware business now rests on the shakiest of foundations, rotten to its core by its illegal reliance on misappropriated trade secrets,” the lawsuit says. Apple said it began investigating whether some of its confidential information was compromised and “uncovered a pattern of theft” of Apple’s trade secrets by former employees who moved on to positions at OpenAI. The lawsuit alleges both Liu and Tan accessed Apple’s confidential company information and files while working at OpenAI. Among the allegations, Apple claims Liu accessed and downloaded several confidential hardware-related files on an Apple-issued device he kept after departing. It also alleges Tan directed job candidates who were still working for Apple to bring “Actual parts” from Apple to their interviews at OpenAI. Apple said in the lawsuit that it reached out to OpenAI in February to raise its concerns early in its investigation, but said that OpenAI did not respond. An Apple spokesperson said in a statement Friday that the company will “always defend our teams’ hard work and innovations, and we are taking all appropriate steps to do so.” A partnership with Apple has edged toward rivalry Apple sought help from OpenAI several years ago as it was behind in the AI race sparked by ChatGPT’s arrival. The two companies partnered in 2024 to use ChatGPT as an AI-powered “answer engine” on the iPhone when the built-in Siri technology couldn’t satisfy user needs. More recently, the partnership has veered toward rivalry. As part of its expansion efforts, OpenAI recruited former Apple designer Jony Ive to oversee a project to build an AI-powered device that many analysts believe could eventually challenge Apple’s products. Last year, OpenAI announced it was working on a secret hardware collaboration with Ive to pioneer a new way of communicating with artificial intelligence. As part of the collaboration, OpenAI acquired io Products, a product and engineering company co-founded by Ive, Tan and two others, in a deal valued at nearly $6.5 billion. That led a little-known tech startup iyO Inc. to sue Ive and OpenAI CEO Sam Altman for trademark infringement due to the similar-sounding name and the firms’ past interactions. The startup also sued one of its own former employees for allegedly leaking a confidential drawing of iyO’s unreleased product, and it later added trade secret theft claims against Tan to the lawsuit. Apple’s lawsuit also names io Products as a defendant. Lawyers who previously represented the firm and Tan referred The Associated Press to OpenAI for comment. Apple’s lawsuit comes as OpenAI has been exploring whether to go public on Wall Street and faces heightened competition from rivals including Anthropic and Google. OpenAI winnowed down some of its business ventures earlier this year to focus on its core product, ChatGPT, but has continued to pursue a device, the company’s chief financial officer told The Associated Press this spring. “We have consumer hardware that will come towards the end of this year,” CFO Sarah Friar told the AP in April.
headlines.source: Kaitlyn Huamani | The Associated Press and Matt O'brien | The Associated Press

headlines.title: Hate crime investigation in Palo Alto in response to vandalism inside public restrooms
headlines.description: Palo Alto police have launched a hate crime investigation after spray-painted swastikas and racial epithets were discovered inside public restrooms at city parks and parking garages, the department announced Friday. The first vandalism case was reported on the Fourth of July after a public works employee found spray-painted swastikas and sexual images inside the men’s restroom at Greer Park, police said. The crime occurred at some point between July 2 and July 4. On Monday, a public works employee found graffiti vandalism inside the men’s restroom at Rinconada Park. Someone used a permanent marker to scrawl graffiti on the walls at some point between June 30 and Monday morning, police said. There was no hate crime component in this particular case, police said. In response to the two cases and another hate crime vandalism case at Rinconada Park from June 11, the public works department decided to inspect other public restrooms across the city. The inspections revealed seven additional hate crime vandalism cases at the locations listed below. All occurred in men’s restrooms and contained racial epithets directed at Black people, police said. Ramos Park (800 East Meadow Dr.) Seale Park (3100 Stockton Pl.) City Parking Garage S/L (445 Bryant St.) Greer Park (1098 Amarillo Ave.) El Camino Park (155 El Camino Real) Heritage Park (300 Homer Ave.) City Parking Garage C-7 (350 Sherman Ave.) Police said they do not know if any or all of the cases are connected or if they were committed by the same person. They also did not have any suspect information but said the investigation is ongoing. Officers are increasing patrols in the areas where vandalism was found and in other locations with similar city facilities, police said. Anyone with information about the vandalism cases is encouraged to call police at 650-329-2413.

headlines.title: Fairfield graduation shooting suspect arrested in Texas
headlines.description: An arrest has been made in connection to last month’s deadly shooting at a high school graduation ceremony in Fairfield, police announced Friday. The June 3 shooting at the Sem Yeto graduation on the Fairfield High School campus left 18-year-old Jamario Baker dead and three others injured. Police said the 17-year-old suspect was taken into custody Friday morning with the help of the United States Marshals Service in the greater Dallas-Forth Worth area in Texas. He will remain in custody pending extradition proceedings to Solano County and is being held on suspicion of murder, according to the Fairfield Police Department. “This arrest represents a significant milestone in an investigation that has required hundreds of hours of investigative work and the coordinated efforts of numerous law enforcement professionals,” police said in a news release. “Investigators conducted dozens of witness interviews, reviewed hours of surveillance and digital video, analyzed photographs and electronic records, executed search warrants, processed forensic evidence, and coordinated efforts across multiple jurisdictions to develop a comprehensive and prosecutable case.” Fairfield Jun 4 Fairfield Jun 5 Fairfield Jun 3 Police also said the shooting investigation remains active, adding detectives continue to pursue leads. Anyone with information about the shooting is urged to contact the Fairfield Police Department Investigations Division at 707-428-7600 or by email at juneincidenttips@fairfield.ca.gov. Fairfield police are expected to hold a news briefing Monday afternoon to discuss the case. https://www.facebook.com/FairfieldPolice/posts/pfbid0gGPn2WgRt4buofmvPct4PASiYE3ovBco9hkP4s9iE7EEM99cXBsobHKJJSAFKE3Nl

headlines.title: Hundreds of tons of raw sugar stalled on Richmond dock as labor dispute drags on
headlines.description: A ship currently docked in Richmond is at the center of a controversy that involves a Bay Area labor dispute, a mass killing in the Philippines, and hundreds of tons of raw sugar stranded on a dock. The raw sugar in question remains on a dock in Richmond while the workers required to transport it are on strike, with no clear end in sight. On Wednesday, members of ILWU Local 10, a union that represents dockworkers, and the Bay Area’s Filipino community rallied at the Levin-Richmond Terminal, where sugar company California & Hawaiian Sugar was attempting to unload a shipment of raw sugar from the Tai Herald, a cargo ship. The ship was originally headed to C&H Sugar’s facility in Crockett. But C&H Sugar warehouse workers represented by ILWU Local 6 have been on strike there since June after contract negotiations fell through over cuts in retirement and healthcare benefits. In solidarity, C&H Sugar dockworkers represented by ILWU Local 10 resolved not to cross the picket line and refused to unload the raw sugar from Tai Herald at the Crockett facility. “It’s a disgraceful thing to cut your retirees’ healthcare,” said Michael Villeggiante, President of the ILWU Local 10. “They work for 30 or 40 years for those benefits, and then you can’t just cut them out!” With the ILWU Local 10 workers unwilling to unload the sugar, the ship was rerouted to the industrial Levin-Richmond Terminal, where it has been docked since July 2. There, Villeggiante said, the company has tapped into workers belonging to a different union, Operating Engineers Local 3, to unload the sugar from the ship onto the dock at Levin-Richmond Terminal. As of Thursday afternoon, Villeggiante said that the ship has been partially unloaded, and some of the raw sugar is now sitting on the terminal. More than 200 people showed up to protest the unloading at Wednesday’s rally, including members of ILWU Local 6 and Local 10, and Richmond elected officials. Also present were members of the Bay Area’s Filipino community, who wanted to shed light on the atrocities faced by farmers in Negros Occidental, the province in the Philippines where the raw sugar came from. The Defend Negros Coalition, comprising Filipino groups from Northern California, helped organize Wednesday’s rally along with ILWU Local 10. Negros Occidental, also known as the country’s sugarcane bowl, is among the poorest provinces of the Philippines, where sugarcane workers work under severely exploitative conditions, according to the coalition. The Defend Negros Coalition was formed after authorities killed 19 people in Negros Island in April. Among them were two Filipino American activists: Lyle Prijoles from Hayward and Kai Sorem from Washington state. “We were alarmed to find out that there was sugar coming to the Bay Area from Negros Island,” said Ricky Pataksil, spokesperson of the Defend Negros Coalition. “Because of the low wages and the conditions that sugarcane workers in Negros face, and the history of the killings and massacres, we believe that there’s very much blood in the sugar that’s being shipped over from the Philippines.” Pataksil said that he and other Filipino activists who attended the rally are supporting the striking ILWU workers to hold C&H Sugar and the government of the Philippines accountable for the sugarcane growers’ exploitation. Then there is the potential threat of contamination of the raw sugar that is being unloaded onto the Levin-Richmond Terminal, which is an industrial terminal. The dock is used to unload “cement, scrap metal, coal, petroleum products, and oil,” said Villegiante. “So, they’re loading the sugar directly onto the ground, high piling it, and it’s being exposed to all the things that I just mentioned.” In response, a C&H Sugar spokesperson said Thursday it’s common practice for raw sugar to be handled via bulk terminals like the Levin Terminal. “In terms of quality, there is no difference in using this terminal or using the refinery’s. Raw sugar is not food grade until it moves through a sugar refinery,” according to a statement from C&H. “Once it has been processed in our refinery, we assure the quality of our finished sugar.” Villegiante added that it would be difficult for the company to transport the unloaded sugar from the port to the facility in Crockett without the labor of the ILWU Local 6 and Local 10. As a result, it remains unclear how long the raw sugar will remain on the dock. “You’re talking hundreds and hundreds of tons of sugar,” he said. “To truck it up there and then to try to put it in some sort of system to get to the silos? That’s going to be a feat by itself.”

headlines.title: Lighting upgrades coming to East Bay BART stations
headlines.description: Leaders on Friday announced new funding to upgrade the lighting at nine East Bay BART stations. BART is receiving $850,000 to install LED lights at the Coliseum, Downtown Berkeley, Fruitvale, Lake Merritt, MacArthur, Rockridge, San Leandro, North Berkeley and West Oakland stations. East Bay Congresswoman Lateefah Simon helped secure the funding for stations in her district. NBC Bay Area’s Bob Redell has more in the video report above.

headlines.title: Flood advisory issued for Bay Area shorelines, coast
headlines.description: The National Weather Service has issued a coastal flood advisory for the greater San Francisco Bay Area and is forecasting up to 1.7 feet of flooding in low-lying areas near bay shorelines and along the coast. The advisory is expected to remain in place through July 19 in shoreline areas of the San Francisco and San Pablo bays. The advisory for coastal areas will begin Saturday and run through July 15. Sneaker waves and strong rip currents are also expected in low-lying areas through 5 p.m. Friday, according to the National Weather Service. The Central Marin Police Department also issued a message on Thursday informing residents that king tides, especially strong tidal effects that cause above-normal water levels, are expected for the shoreline areas of Central Marin. The king tides are expected to be “higher-than-normal,” according to police officials. With flood conditions in effect, residents are advised to avoid waterside infrastructure and allow extra time for travel on shoreline routes in case of flooded roads. The National Weather Service is also monitoring the effect that recent increases in atmospheric moisture will have on the potential for wildfires throughout the Bay Area both this weekend and early next week. The weather pattern creates favorable conditions for dry thunderstorms absent of precipitation, which combined with gusty winds can start and spread wildfires. Outside of flood conditions, shoreline areas in San Francisco and Oakland should expect mostly highs in the mid 60s that will creep up into the low 70s as the weekend progresses. Inland areas should expect highs in the mid 80s, that will increase into the low 90s in areas around Livermore, Santa Rosa and Stockton over the weekend. Throughout the entire Bay Area, overnight lows in the 50s are forecasted for the weekend.

headlines.title: Man dies after crash on Interstate 80 in Fairfield area
headlines.description: Speed may have played a role in a deadly crash on Interstate 80 in the Fairfield area early Friday morning, according to the California Highway Patrol. The crash happened on westbound I-80 near Red Top Road, the CHP said. An approximately 60-year-old Emeryville man behind the wheel of a gray Lexus sedan allegedly was speeding when he was involved in a minor crash with a black Lexus sedan, according to the CHP. The impact caused the gray Lexus to hit the center median barrier, overturn and come to rest in the far left lane. The driver of a gray Dodge Durango subsequently crashed into the gray Lexus. The driver of the gray Lexus died at the scene, the CHP said. No other injuries were reported. The CHP said speed appeared to be a factor in the crash, but the exact cause remains under investigation. All westbound I-80 lanes reopened at about 5:50 a.m., according to the CHP.

headlines.title: Gas prices in the Bay Area rise slightly as U.S. and Iran exchange attacks
headlines.description: The statewide gas average went slightly up Thursday, as the United States and Iran trade new attacks. After weeks of slightly lower prices following President Donald Trump’s announced ceasefire, AAA says California’s statewide average ticked back up overnight. The price increase comes as new video shows a fresh round of U.S. strikes on Iran after the Iran’s recent attacks on commercial ships in the Strait of Hormuz. “We just hit them very hard, and I say we hit up 20 to one every time they hit us, we’re going to hit them 20,” Trump said. For months, Trump said Iran was prepared to reach a deal. Now, he’s not so sure. “I just don’t know if they’re worthy of making a deal. I don’t know that they’re going to honor the deal, that’s the problem,” he said. “The problem with the memorandum of understanding is that it was pretty vague, there were a lot of things to be settled,” Professor Stephen Zunes, director of Middle Eastern Studies at the University of San Francisco, said. Zunes believes the conflict could drag on for months, making it harder to bring prices back down. “What I fear here frankly is that this is going to be the kind of thing we’re going to be seeing going on for weeks, or even months or even longer.” And he says the impact could reach far beyond the gas pump. Higher fuel costs can make it more expensive to transport goods, and drive up farming costs, which can lead to higher grocery bills. “The farmers, who have to pay more for fertilizer, fuel for their tractors, higher food prices,” he said. “The economic impacts are going to continue to worsen.” They’re impacts that many families locally can’t afford.

headlines.title: 16 abandoned puppies rescued by good Samaritans in Livermore
headlines.description: Sixteen abandoned puppies will have a new chance to find forever homes thanks to the actions of good Samaritans in Livermore. Breann, who declined to share her last name, regularly helps catch stray and lost dogs in the Tri-Valley. She said she got a call on Wednesday from someone who was in the grassy creek bed behind the Livermore Walmart parking lot and found a group of puppies. It turned out to be a large group of 17 puppies, but one of those puppies was hit and killed by a car before help could arrive. Breann went over to the site and brought a friend in an effort to corral the puppies. “They had big, wormy bellies, and it really broke my heart because you could tell they were skinny and they were just bloat[ed] for the most part,” she said. Breann and the other good Samaritans helping out loaded up the sixteen puppies into two cars. “It’s definitely a lot of puppies all at once, it kind of felt like it was never gonna end,” she recalled. “When we were putting one by one into the truck, I was like, ‘And there’s more!’” Breann said she wasn’t surprised to see this abandoned group and that she’s seen more and more abandoned animals in the Tri-Valley as the cost of spaying and neutering rises. The good Samaritans brought these puppies to East Bay SPCA, which has the contract with the city of Livermore to take in the city’s lost, injured, or stray animals. East Bay SPCA brought in six of the puppies to its Dublin location and had the good Samaritans bring the other ten puppies to their Oakland location. “It’s no small feat,” said Jes Cytron, Vice President of Operations for East Bay SPCA. But Cytron said the puppies have acclimated to life in the shelter so far. Cytron believes the puppies are border collie mixes that are nine to ten weeks old, likely from two different litters. “People are really lacking the resources they need to take care of accidental or unwanted litters, often times they may panic, they may not know we’re here to take in those animals,” Cytron said. East Bay SPCA estimates these puppies will be ready for adoption in about a week. Anyone interested in adopting one should check the East Bay SPCA website, which will be updated when the puppies are available.

headlines.title: YMCA summer camp counselor accused of sexually assaulting girl in Morgan Hill
headlines.description: A YMCA summer camp counselor was arrested Thursday after allegedly sexually assaulting a 10-year-old girl, Morgan Hill police said. Police said they received a report regarding a 10-year-old girl who had been sexually assaulted by a YMCA summer camp counselor on June 30. Authorities said the incident occurred at a summer day camp at the Morgan Hill Aquatic Center. The counselor was identified as Anthony Joseph Ramon, 25, of Gilroy, according to authorities. They said Ramon was immediately placed on leave. Anthony Joseph Ramon. Courtesy of Morgan Hill PD. On Thursday, Ramon was taken into custody on suspicion of lewd and lascivious acts on a minor. Officials said Ramon had been working with YMCA since Aug. 6, 2024. Authorities encourage anyone with information to contact Detective Sergeant Sean Bayard at 408-607-3032 or email at sean.bayard@morganhill.ca.gov.

headlines.title: Family and friends looking for missing San Francisco man
headlines.description: Friends and family are looking for Gabriel Angelo Carreon, a San Francisco man who went missing. According to his husband, Carreon was last seen Tuesday in the Castro neighborhood. Gabriel Carreon’s husband, Ryan Mahaffey, said the last time Carreon was seen was Tuesday morning in the Castro neighborhood. According to San Francisco police, Carreon was on his way to see a movie. Mahaffey says they’ve called 20 different hotels in the city, checked San Francisco hospitals and even checked with an immigration attorney friend to see if he may have been detained by ICE. Carreon is a legal resident with a green card; Mahaffey says he’s not in ICE custody, so his friends have launched a full-on search, including a Facebook page and sharing those “missing” posters. “He’s about 5 foot 7. 170 pounds. He currently has pink hair. He was last seen headed toward the Castro Muni station wearing a backpack and possibly a red baseball cap,” John Shinogle, a friend, said. Shinogle is one of the friends helping with the search. He was also the best man in Carreon and Mahaffey’s wedding last year. He says Carreon is very active in neighborhood sports teams and organizations. Mahaffey said he became concerned Tuesday, when Carreon didn’t show up for an afternoon doctor’s appointment. Right now, they just want him to be found safe. “We love you and we want to bring you home safely and we’re working tirelessly to do so,” Shinogle said. Anyone with information is encouraged to call San Francisco police, call 650-701-3540 or log onto the Facebook page.
